## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Docket:
  ## Character
  
  First end-to-end execution of the gauntlet suite via rbw-tP against actual fresh state. The bug class this heat exists to catch — silent first-build assumptions — surfaces here in its remaining form: case-level probes have caught what they can during walkthrough and fixture construction; this pace exposes the cross-section bugs only the full assembled sequence reveals.
  
  **Wrap criterion is green tally.** Not "ran to completion." Not "captured findings." Not "construction complete." This pace exists as a structural guardrail against the recurring agent failure mode where wrap is driven at "I built the thing" rather than "the thing actually works against reality." If the first run surfaces issues, fixes land here (small) or spawn follow-up paces (large) — but this pace stays open until rbw-tP runs green end-to-end.
  
  Intent-only docket. Triage decisions surface at mount time per surprise.
  
  Post-BBABB landing: this pace may run concurrently with BBABA on a different machine when both stations carry distinct BURS_TINCTURE values. Tincture composition makes RBDC-derived names disjoint per station; the remaining collision surface is operator error (same tincture on both stations).
  
  ## Cost
  
  ~1 hour wall-clock + 2 GCP projects per attempt (one throwaway from pristine-lifecycle fixture, one canonical from canonical-establish). Re-runs after inline fixes accumulate cost; budget accordingly.
  
  ## Scope
  
  - Marshal zero per RELEASE.md §2 (signature commit on HEAD)
  - Run rbw-tP, which enforces entry contract and exec's the gauntlet suite tabtarget
  - Capture every surprise — failed assertions, unexpected diagnostics, performance anomalies, missing tabtargets, awkward UX
  - Triage routing per surprise:
    - Tiny inline (one-line bash, message clarity) — fix and re-run within this pace
    - Larger (structural, design call) — slate as follow-up pace, surface to operator before deciding
  - Record into pace closure: wall-clock total, per-phase wall-clock, cloud cost, surprises + dispositions
  
  ## Collision-aware diagnostic
  
  Any GCP "resource already exists" or equivalent name-collision error during the run produces a diagnostic that names BURS_TINCTURE and the colliding term, with a hypothesis pointing at peer-station tincture overlap. Distinguishes tincture-composition collision from transient GCP race.
  
  ## Constraints
  
  - Honor paddock's Release-branch execution contract
  - **Wrap criterion is green tally** — explicit guardrail, restated to defeat drift
  - Re-runs within the pace are first-class; each re-run draws fresh quota
  - Per BBAAN's invest-fatal-on-exists and divest-deletes-production-RBRA: any retry path must respect cult-verb posture; reset between attempts goes through marshal-zero, not partial recovery
  - Per RELEASE.md failure mode contract: mid-qualification failure means start over from marshal-zero, not patch-and-continue
  
  ## Cross-pace dependencies
  
  - gauntlet-suite-assembly landed (rbw-tP and the gauntlet suite tabtarget exist)
  - BBABB landed (BURS_TINCTURE composition; required for the parallel-station property)
  
  ## Out of scope
  
  - Restoring RBRAs — gauntlet self-mints all role credentials (BBAAN's invest cult-verb); only Payor OAuth is the operator's prerequisite
  - /rbk-prep-release integration (BBAAH)
  - Multi-day re-run scenarios
  - Cleanup beyond what rbw-tP itself does — operator peeks post-success and runs cleanup tabtargets manually if desired

Docket:
  ## Character
  
  First end-to-end execution of the gauntlet suite via rbw-tP against actual fresh state. The bug class this heat exists to catch — silent first-build assumptions — surfaces here in its remaining form: case-level probes have caught what they can during walkthrough and fixture construction; this pace exposes the cross-section bugs only the full assembled sequence reveals.
  
  **Wrap criterion is green tally.** Not "ran to completion." Not "captured findings." Not "construction complete." This pace exists as a structural guardrail against the recurring agent failure mode where wrap is driven at "I built the thing" rather than "the thing actually works against reality." If the first run surfaces issues, fixes land here (small) or spawn follow-up paces (large) — but this pace stays open until rbw-tP runs green end-to-end.
  
  Intent-only docket. Triage decisions surface at mount time per surprise.
  
  Post-BBABB landing: this pace may run concurrently with BBABA on a different machine when both stations carry distinct BURS_TINCTURE values. Tincture composition makes RBDC-derived names disjoint per station; the remaining collision surface is operator error (same tincture on both stations).
  
  ## Cost
  
  ~1 hour wall-clock + 2 GCP projects per attempt (one throwaway from pristine-lifecycle fixture, one canonical from canonical-establish). Re-runs after inline fixes accumulate cost; budget accordingly.
  
  ## Scope
  
  - Marshal zero per RELEASE.md §2 (signature commit on HEAD)
  - Run rbw-tP, which enforces entry contract and exec's the gauntlet suite tabtarget
  - Capture every surprise — failed assertions, unexpected diagnostics, performance anomalies, missing tabtargets, awkward UX
  - Triage routing per surprise:
    - Tiny inline (one-line bash, message clarity) — fix and re-run within this pace
    - Larger (structural, design call) — slate as follow-up pace, surface to operator before deciding
  - Record into pace closure: wall-clock total, per-phase wall-clock, cloud cost, surprises + dispositions
  
  ## Collision-aware diagnostic
  
  Any GCP "resource already exists" or equivalent name-collision error during the run produces a diagnostic that names BURS_TINCTURE and the colliding term, with a hypothesis pointing at peer-station tincture overlap. Distinguishes tincture-composition collision from transient GCP race.
  
  ## Constraints
  
  - Honor paddock's Release-branch execution contract
  - **Wrap criterion is green tally** — explicit guardrail, restated to defeat drift
  - Re-runs within the pace are first-class; each re-run draws fresh quota
  - Per BBAAN's invest-fatal-on-exists and divest-deletes-production-RBRA: any retry path must respect cult-verb posture; reset between attempts goes through marshal-zero, not partial recovery
  - Per RELEASE.md failure mode contract: mid-qualification failure means start over from marshal-zero, not patch-and-continue
  
  ## Cross-pace dependencies
  
  - gauntlet-suite-assembly landed (rbw-tP and the gauntlet suite tabtarget exist)
  - BBABB landed (BURS_TINCTURE composition; required for the parallel-station property)
  
  ## Out of scope
  
  - Restoring RBRAs — gauntlet self-mints all role credentials (BBAAN's invest cult-verb); only Payor OAuth is the operator's prerequisite
  - /rbk-prep-release integration (BBAAH)
  - Multi-day re-run scenarios
  - Cleanup beyond what rbw-tP itself does — operator peeks post-success and runs cleanup tabtargets manually if desired

    Silks: rbtd-fixture-nomenclature-collapse

    ## Character
    
    Two-step nomenclature simplification in the rbtd fixture engine.
    Mechanical refactor with one careful read of test-coverage impact.
    
    ## Goals
    
    1. Eliminate "case name string" as a parallel surface to function name.
       Today `case!()` already sets name from `stringify!($func)`, so case
       names ARE function names — but operator-facing surfaces (commit
       prose, dockets, error messages) treat the kebab form as if it were
       a key. Make canonical = function name; either drop kebab from
       user-facing output entirely, or accept kebab as an alias resolver.
    
    2. Eliminate `rbtdre_Section` abstraction. Replace with flat
       `cases: &[Case]` directly on `rbtdre_Fixture`. Fixture-level
       fail-fast vs keep-going (already controlled by disposition) is the
       only granularity actually wanted. Multi-section fixtures
       (enrollment-validation, regime-validation) absorb their grouping
       into case-name prefixes if helpful for display.
    
    ## Wrap criterion
    
    - All sections collapsed; `rbtdre_Section` struct removed.
    - `calibrant-fail-fast` inter-section coverage retired or repurposed
      (it currently exists to test the abstraction being removed).
    - All fixtures still pass under their existing tabtargets.
    
    ## Out of scope
    
    - Renaming individual case functions for prose readability.
    - Changes to fixture lifecycle (setup/teardown), dispositions,
      or run/list tabtarget surfaces.
    - Anything in rbtw or upstream of the engine API.

content.text:
**Pace goal**: Collapse two parallel surfaces in the rbtd fixture engine — make case-function-names canonical (drop kebab as a separate key), and flatten `rbtdre_Section` so fixtures hold `cases: &[Case]` directly.

**Heat context**: ₣BB rbk-mvp-3-release-qualification — 46/57 done. This is an engine-cleanup pace amid release qualification work; BBAAX (first end-to-end rbw-tP run) is the headline pace.

**Silks check**: `rbtd-fixture-nomenclature-collapse` fits — names the two collapses (case name + section). No rename needed.

**Approach**:
- **Step 1 — case-name collapse**: Survey `case!()` macro and find where kebab strings surface (run output, fixture-case dispatch, error messages). Decide: drop kebab entirely from user-facing output, OR keep kebab as a thin alias resolver into function names. Operator-facing surfaces (commit prose, dockets) shift to function names.
- **Step 2 — Section collapse**: Replace `rbtdre_Section` with flat `cases: &[Case]` on `rbtdre_Fixture`. Multi-section fixtures (enrollment-validation, regime-validation) absorb grouping into case-name prefixes. Retire `calibrant-fail-fast` coverage (tests the dying abstraction).
- **Verify**: `tt/rbtd-b.Build.sh` + `tt/rbtd-t.Test.sh` + relevant fixture runs (enrollment-validation, regime-validation, regime-smoke).

**Execution strategy**: Sequential opus — Rust refactor with cross-cutting surface implications (engine, fixture defs, run tabtargets, single-case dispatch). Step 1 needs a judgment call (drop vs alias); worth surfacing the survey before committing.

**Question before proceeding**: Step 1 has two doors — fully drop kebab from user-facing output, or keep it as an alias resolver into function names. Want me to survey current kebab surfaces first and surface the trade before deciding, or do you have a preference?

### Test Sections

Bottle/sentry security cases — shared verbatim by the `tadmor` and `moriah` fixtures (`RBTDRC_SECTIONS_SECURITY` in `rbtdrc_crucible.rs`). The two fixtures differ only in provenance: tadmor runs against a locally-kludged bottle, moriah against the cloud-built airgap bottle. Runtime semantics are identical.

| Section | Pattern | Example |
|---------|---------|---------|
| `basic-infra` | Smoke tests — containers up, DNS responding | pentacle ping, dnsmasq responds |
| `ifrit-attacks` | Single ifrit attack, verdict from inside only | dns-allowed, dns-blocked, apt blocked |
| `observation` | Sentry-side observation of bottle behavior | iptables loaded, blocked-with-observation |
| `correlated` | Theurge resolves on sentry, ifrit attacks with result | tcp443 allow/block, ICMP hop tests |

### Test Sections

Bottle/sentry security cases — shared verbatim by the `tadmor` and `moriah` fixtures (`RBTDRC_SECTIONS_SECURITY` in `rbtdrc_crucible.rs`). The two fixtures differ only in provenance: tadmor runs against a locally-kludged bottle, moriah against the cloud-built airgap bottle. Runtime semantics are identical.

| Section | Pattern | Example |
|---------|---------|---------|
| `basic-infra` | Smoke tests — containers up, DNS responding | pentacle ping, dnsmasq responds |
| `ifrit-attacks` | Single ifrit attack, verdict from inside only | dns-allowed, dns-blocked, apt blocked |
| `observation` | Sentry-side observation of bottle behavior | iptables loaded, blocked-with-observation |
| `correlated` | Theurge resolves on sentry, ifrit attacks with result | tcp443 allow/block, ICMP hop tests |
| `sortie-attacks` | Multi-step ifrit sorties (complex attack sequences) | DNS exfil, metadata probe, raw socket smuggle |
| `unilateral-novel` | Ifrit sorties testing novel attack vectors | route manipulation, subnet escape, DNAT reflection |
| `coordinated-attacks` | Simultaneous attack + observation | ARP gratuitous/poison, table stability |
| `coordinated-integrity` | Attack then verify sentry state unchanged | sentry integrity, DNS cache integrity, MAC flood |

### Adding a New Test

**New ifrit attack** (simple probe, single command):
1. Add constant, enum variant, `from_selector`/`selector`/`all_selectors` entries in `rbida_attacks.rs`
2. Add dispatch arm in `rbida_run()`
3. Add theurge case function calling `rbtdrc_invoke_ifrit(ctx, "selector-name", dir)`
4. Register in appropriate section

**New ifrit sortie** (complex multi-step attack):
1. Add `pub fn sortie_name()` in `rbida_sorties.rs`
2. Add constant, enum variant, and dispatch in `rbida_attacks.rs` (same as above)
3. Add theurge case — sorties may need coordinated observation (writ/fiat before/after)
4. Register in appropriate section

**Crucible verification workflow** (applies to all new tests and changes):

1. Build theurge: `tt/rbtd-b.Build.sh` and run unit tests: `tt/rbtd-t.Test.sh`
2. If ifrit source changed: kludge-rebuild, commit hallmark, then charge:
   ```
   tt/rbw-cKB.KludgeBottle.sh tadmor    # builds image, drives hallmark into rbrn.env

The two fixtures differ only in provenance: tadmor runs against a locally-kludged bottle, moriah against the cloud-built airgap bottle. Runtime semantics are identical.

Cases group by purpose in source order (no formal section structure):

- **basic-infra** — smoke tests: pentacle ping, dnsmasq responds
- **ifrit-attacks** — single ifrit attack, verdict from inside only: dns-allowed/blocked, apt blocked
- **observation** — sentry-side observation of bottle behavior: iptables loaded, blocked-with-observation
- **correlated** — theurge resolves on sentry, ifrit attacks with result: tcp443 allow/block, ICMP hop tests
- **sortie-attacks** — multi-step ifrit sorties: DNS exfil, metadata probe, raw socket smuggle

- **coordinated-attacks** — simultaneous attack + observation: ARP gratuitous/poison, table stability

1. Add constant, enum variant, `from_selector`/`selector`/`all_selectors` entries in `rbida_attacks.rs`
2. Add dispatch arm in `rbida_run()`
3. Add theurge case function calling `rbtdrc_invoke_ifrit(ctx, "selector-name", dir)`
4. Register in `RBTDRC_CASES_SECURITY` (or the relevant fixture's case array)

## Theurge & Ifrit: Crucible Security Testing

Read this file when working on theurge (test orchestrator), ifrit (attack binary), or crucible test cases. This is Recipe Bottle's internal test infrastructure only — Recipe Bottle does not require users to have Rust compilation tools.

### Architecture

Two Rust binaries with completely different roles and build targets:

- **Theurge** (`Tools/rbk/rbtd/`) — test orchestrator, runs on the **host** (macOS/Linux). Charges a crucible (sentry + pentacle + bottle containers), invokes attacks, observes results, produces verdicts. Built via `tt/rbtd-b.Build.sh`.
- **Ifrit** (`rbev-vessels/common-ifrit-context/`) — attack binary, runs **inside the bottle container**. Probes network security boundaries from the attacker's perspective. Source lives in the shared build context consumed by `rbev-bottle-ifrit-tether` (and the forthcoming airgap variant). Built inside the Docker image during `docker build` — there is no host-side compilation, no cross-compile, no `cargo check` on macOS. The Dockerfile IS the build system.

**Coordinated tests** are the distinctive capability: theurge simultaneously observes from outside (via sentry writ/fiat commands) while ifrit attacks from inside. Neither binary alone can do this.

### Crucible Iteration Loop

The typical development cycle when changing ifrit or theurge code:

#### Iteration strategy: single cases first, full suite last

The full tadmor fixture takes ~10 minutes (charge + 50+ cases + quench). **Do not run it as the first verification step** — whether debugging failures or verifying new code. Instead:

1. Charge the crucible once
2. Run single-case against each new or changed test to verify it passes
3. Run the full fixture only after all targeted cases pass — as a final regression check
4. Quench

This applies to **all** crucible verification: new tests, bug fixes, refactors. The full suite is always the last step, never the first.

#### Full run (charge + all cases + quench in one command)

1. Edit ifrit source (`rbev-vessels/common-ifrit-context/src/`) or theurge source (`Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/`)
2. If ifrit changed: kludge-rebuild the bottle image
   ```
   tt/rbw-cKB.KludgeBottle.sh tadmor
   ```
   This builds a new container image and drives the kludge hallmark into the nameplate's `rbrn.env`.
3. Git commit the hallmark change (kludge dirties `rbrn.env` — a clean working tree is required for charge, and the commit trail maintains audit integrity)
4. Run the full tadmor fixture:
   ```
   tt/rbtd-r.FixtureRun.tadmor.sh
   ```
   This charges the crucible, runs all cases, and quenches — one command.

#### Single-case debugging (manual charge/quench lifecycle)

When iterating on a specific failing test case:

1. Charge the crucible (starts containers, leaves them running):
   ```
   tt/rbw-cC.Charge.tadmor.sh
   ```
2. Run individual cases against the live crucible:
   ```
   tt/rbtd-s.FixtureCase.tadmor.sh case-name
   ```
   Omit the case name to list all available cases.
3. Edit code, rebuild as needed (kludge for ifrit, `tt/rbtd-b.Build.sh` for theurge), re-run the single case. Repeat.
4. Quench when done:
   ```
   tt/rbw-cQ.Quench.tadmor.sh
   ```

#### Ordaining after green

Kludge builds are for rapid local iteration. Once all tests pass with the kludge hallmark, ordain for a production-grade Cloud Build image:

1. Ordain the vessel:
   ```
   tt/rbw-fO.DirectorOrdainsHallmark.sh rbev-bottle-ifrit-tether
   ```
2. Summon the ordained hallmark locally:
   ```
   tt/rbw-fs.RetrieverSummonsHallmark.sh rbev-bottle-ifrit-tether <hallmark>
   ```
3. Drive the ordained hallmark into the nameplate (edit `RBRN_BOTTLE_HALLMARK` in `.rbk/tadmor/rbrn.env`), commit, and re-run the full fixture to verify.

**Hallmark prefixes** tell you what you have: `k` = kludge (local build), `c` = conjured (Cloud Build ordained).

### Test Sections

Bottle/sentry security cases — shared verbatim by the `tadmor` and `moriah` fixtures (`RBTDRC_SECTIONS_SECURITY` in `rbtdrc_crucible.rs`). The two fixtures differ only in provenance: tadmor runs against a locally-kludged bottle, moriah against the cloud-built airgap bottle. Runtime semantics are identical.

| Section | Pattern | Example |
|---------|---------|---------|
| `basic-infra` | Smoke tests — containers up, DNS responding | pentacle ping, dnsmasq responds |
| `ifrit-attacks` | Single ifrit attack, verdict from inside only | dns-allowed, dns-blocked, apt blocked |
| `observation` | Sentry-side observation of bottle behavior | iptables loaded, blocked-with-observation |
| `correlated` | Theurge resolves on sentry, ifrit attacks with result | tcp443 allow/block, ICMP hop tests |
| `sortie-attacks` | Multi-step ifrit sorties (complex attack sequences) | DNS exfil, metadata probe, raw socket smuggle |
| `unilateral-novel` | Ifrit sorties testing novel attack vectors | route manipulation, subnet escape, DNAT reflection |
| `coordinated-attacks` | Simultaneous attack + observation | ARP gratuitous/poison, table stability |
| `coordinated-integrity` | Attack then verify sentry state unchanged | sentry integrity, DNS cache integrity, MAC flood |

### Adding a New Test

**New ifrit attack** (simple probe, single command):
1. Add constant, enum variant, `from_selector`/`selector`/`all_selectors` entries in `rbida_attacks.rs`
2. Add dispatch arm in `rbida_run()`
3. Add theurge case function calling `rbtdrc_invoke_ifrit(ctx, "selector-name", dir)`
4. Register in appropriate section

**New ifrit sortie** (complex multi-step attack):
1. Add `pub fn sortie_name()` in `rbida_sorties.rs`
2. Add constant, enum variant, and dispatch in `rbida_attacks.rs` (same as above)
3. Add theurge case — sorties may need coordinated observation (writ/fiat before/after)
4. Register in appropriate section

**Crucible verification workflow** (applies to all new tests and changes):

1. Build theurge: `tt/rbtd-b.Build.sh` and run unit tests: `tt/rbtd-t.Test.sh`
2. If ifrit source changed: kludge-rebuild, commit hallmark, then charge:
   ```
   tt/rbw-cKB.KludgeBottle.sh tadmor    # builds image, drives hallmark into rbrn.env
   # commit the rbrn.env change (clean tree required for charge)
   tt/rbw-cC.Charge.tadmor.sh
   ```
3. Verify each new/changed case individually against the live crucible:
   ```
   tt/rbtd-s.FixtureCase.tadmor.sh rbtdrc_sortie_new_case_name
   ```
4. Only after all targeted cases pass, run the full fixture for regression:
   ```
   tt/rbtd-r.FixtureRun.tadmor.sh
   ```
   Note: this charges and quenches internally — quench the manual crucible first if one is active (`tt/rbw-cQ.Quench.tadmor.sh`).

content.text:
Refactor complete: build clean, 94/94 unit tests pass, fast suite 98/98 pass, doc updated. Pace BBAAy work is at wrap-ready state (pending operator decision).

---

**Re: Rust `assert!` semantics — your C/C++ concern doesn't apply.**

Rust's `assert!` and `assert_eq!`/`assert_ne!` are **always active** in both debug and release builds. They panic on failure unconditionally. There is no `NDEBUG`-equivalent that elides them — they are not conditional on optimization level or build profile. So using them in production paths is safe in the sense you cared about: no hidden failure mode.

The conditional-debug analog in Rust is the separate family `debug_assert!`/`debug_assert_eq!`/`debug_assert_ne!`. Those compile to a no-op under `--release` (specifically when `cfg(debug_assertions)` is false, which release builds turn off by default). That's the family that matches your C `assert` instinct — and the one to be wary of for invariant checks you want enforced in production.

In our test code (`rbtdt*` files and the calibrant tests I just touched), every `assert!` / `assert_eq!` is inside `#[test]` functions — they run under `cargo test`, never in production binaries at all, so the debug/release distinction is moot there. The production binaries (`rbtd`, `rbtdri_invoke_global`, etc.) use `Result<_, String>` returns and explicit `return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(...)` for runtime failure paths — no asserts on hot paths I'm aware of.

Quick check on whether we have stray `debug_assert!` in non-test source:

input.description: Check for debug_assert in production code

content.content: (Bash completed with no output)

content.text:
None. So in this codebase, every `assert!` you see is either (a) in a `#[test]` (only runs under `cargo test`) or (b) a true panic-on-failure invariant that fires in release. Both are deterministic — no hidden-failure C-style trap.

The one Rust idiom that *can* surprise people coming from C: `unwrap()` and `expect()` on `Option`/`Result` panic in both debug and release, just like `assert!`. They're stronger guarantees than C `assert`, not weaker.
